title: Clout to over-65s ‘a blow to budget’
full_text: Other Editions 24 April 2026 TheNation Clout to over-65s ‘a blow to budget’ The Australian NATASHA ROBINSON, LILY McCAFFREY 24 April 2026 1382 words English AUSTLN 6 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Health Minister Mark Butler’s decision to axe higher private health insurance rebates for over-65s, triggering widespread anger among retirees, runs contrary to previous advice to the federal department that found the policy was “good value for government”. The conclusion is in contrast to Mr Butler’s remarks des­cribing higher rebates to older people as “unfair” and without policy merit. Actuarial modelling commissioned by the federal health department under the previous Liberal government and received in 2023 found the policy would cost the federal government more than it saved. The report by consultancy firm Finity found reducing the private health insurance rebate levels for older people would have “a short-term negative ­impact on government finances” as rebate savings would be less than the claims funded by insurance companies for people in this age group. Those without private cover would have to be treated in the public system. The health department held consultations in 2023 arising from the report, indicating the Albanese government then took the findings into consideration. At present, people over 65 in eligible income brackets are given a higher rebate by the government to help pay the cost of private health insurance premiums. The loading is higher still for those aged over 70. Scrapping higher private health insurance rebates will punish older people relying solely on the pension the most, at a time when they are least able to fund private cover. Health insurer peak body Private Healthcare Australia has issued a plea to the government to reconsider the policy for the lowest income earners over 65. The government plans to redirect savings as a result of the axing of the rebates to aged care. The scrapping of higher rebates for older Australians when they need them the most has sparked outrage among many older Australians who have paid private health premiums for much of their working lives while rarely claiming. Retired pharmacist Lisa Willis, 67, said the government’s move “really stinks”. “I’ve had private health insurance for over 40 years,” Ms Willis said. “My husband’s just been diagnosed with Parkinson’s. We’re going to continue on with private health insurance, but I think a rebate continuing would have been nice. I’m very disappointed because I’ve voted Labor for quite a long while.” Private health insurance rebates are means tested according to four income tiers. The base tier income level gets the higher rebate, and the rebate lessens proportionately in tiers 1 and 2, while families with a combined income of more than $316,000 are not eligible for any rebate. Currently, singles aged between 65 and 69 who have an ­income of less than $101,000 receive a rebate towards the cost of their health insurance premium of 28.33 per cent, The rebate also applies to someone over 65 who is in a family with an income of less than $202,000. The rebate is higher for those aged over 70 in this income tier, rising to 32.38 per cent. Those under 65 receive a rebate of 24.3 per cent. The rebate in tier 1 income level is 20.24 per cent for over-65s, 24.28 per cent for the over-70s, and 16.1 per cent for under-65s. At the National Press Club on Wednesday, when he announced the changes, Mr Butler described higher PHI rebates for older people – introduced by the Howard government over a decade ago – as unfair and without policy merit. “It makes no sense from an equity point of view for government to pay a higher level of support to one household than they do on the other, where they have exactly the same income but happen to be of different ages,” he told the National Press Club this week. The minister said scrapping the higher rebates for older people would have “a very, very minimal impact” on older people dumping private health cover. The measure has been estimated to result in premium increases of 5 per cent for those over 65 and 11 per cent for over-70s. The Finity report, which was commissioned before Labor was elected to government but published in 2023, backs Mr Butler’s assertion that a lower PHI rebate for older people would not result in a significant flight of consumers from the private system. But the budget impact would be worse for the government than paying higher rebates. “Economic testing suggests PHI is price-inelastic, so a lower PHI rebate has only a small impact on participation,” the report says. “However, the impact on claims is material, because older people have high average claim costs. “As well as reducing PHI affordability, we expect this would have a short-term negative impact on government finances, as the PHI rebate savings ($0.4bn) are less than the claims funded ($0.4bn-$0.5bn).” The Finity report uses different assumptions and data than the federal government has relied upon to make its decision to scrap older Australians’ higher rebates, including about participation, claims values and rebate expenditure. Nevertheless, it raises questions about the budget impact of axing the age group’s rebates. The Finity report found the federal government would save $482m a year if it were to scrap higher rebates for older people. But the report found such action by the government would result in a total of $547m in fewer private health claims made by this age group. Assuming that this cost were shifted to the public hospital system, it means taxpayers would be paying more for the healthcare of older people who may scrap private health policies than they do for the rebates that currently subsidise their private health cover. “The PHI rebate for over-65s provides good value for government,” the report concludes. Mr Butler said being subject to a lower rebate would add less than $1 a day on to older Australians’ premium costs. “Giving two households on the same income different levels of government support, based only on their age, is not fair between generations and is not the best way to spend precious taxpayers’ dollars on behalf of older Australians when we need to do so much heavy lifting in aged care,” Mr Butler said. “Departmental modelling indicates that the average increase in costs for affected individuals – of less than $1 per day – is likely to have a minimal impact on PHI participation, which is expected to continue growing. While some people may choose to exit PHI because of this change, departmental modelling suggests this is very small (around 0.4 per cent, or 44,000) of the projected insured population in 2028-29). Older Australians will still receive a rebate – of between 8 per cent and 24 per cent depending on income – the same as everyone else.” But people aged over 65 said younger people were still earning an income while older Australians who received the higher, means-tested rebate, did not have disposable cash. Chris Steinback, 73, from Melbourne, said the government’s policy was a “classic case of short-term thinking that’s going to create long-term pain”. He said he and his wife, Carol, were pensioners and like many older Australians had always tried to “do the right thing” by maintaining private health insurance. They have gold level coverage with Medibank with a monthly premium of $453.90 pre-April 1. “But the changes to the private health insurance rebate for people over 65 will increase our premiums. For pensioners, every dollar matters. These increases will force us and many others our age to seriously reconsider whether we can keep our cover,” he said. “If large numbers of seniors cancel their policies because they can’t afford them, health funds will lose the very age group that relies most on care. That will push premiums up even further for everyone who remains.” Opposition health spokesperson Anne Ruston has described the scrapping of higher rebates for older Australians as a “false economy” that would heap pressure on public hospitals, while NSW Premier Chris Minns said policy changes “made by somebody in a darkened room in Canberra” can have seismic impacts on overloaded state hospitals. summary: The Australian government has decided to eliminate higher private health insurance rebates for individuals over 65, despite previous actuarial advice indicating that the policy was financially beneficial for the government. This change is expected to negatively impact older Australians, particularly those on pensions, as it may lead to increased public healthcare costs. The health minister argues that the current rebate system is inequitable, but the financial implications of this decision raise concerns about its long-term effects on government budgets.

key_points: The government is scrapping higher private health insurance rebates for over-65s, citing equity concerns.
Actuarial modelling suggests this decision may lead to higher public healthcare costs than the savings from the rebates.
The change is expected to disproportionately affect low-income retirees who rely on private health insurance.
relevance: This decision impacts the financial landscape for retirees and may influence future actuarial assessments related to healthcare costs and insurance pricing.
